elements.label: text

elements.text: reinsurance arrangement.

elements.text: Note:
elements.label: text

elements.label: text

elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae or to become a lien on the property that is superior to Fannie Mae's lien.

elements.text: B7-3-02, Property Insurance Requirements for One-to Four-Unit Properties (02/07/2024)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on property insurance requirements for one- to four-unit properties, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Coverage Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Determining the Required Coverage Amount
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Deductible Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Coverage Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Property insurance policies for one- to four-unit properties securing loans purchased by Fannie Mae should be written on a "Special" coverage form or equivalent. At a minimum, the coverage must include the perils listed in the following table.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Required Perils
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Fire or lightning
elements.label: text

elements.text: Explosion
elements.label: text

elements.text: Windstorm (including named storms designated by the U.S. National Weather Service or the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration by a name or number)
elements.label: text

elements.text: Hail
elements.label: text

elements.text: Smoke
elements.label: text

elements.text: Aircraft
elements.label: text

elements.text: Vehicles
elements.label: text

elements.text: Riot or civil commotion
elements.label: text

elements.text: If a property insurance policy excludes or limits coverage of any of the required perils, the borrower must obtain an acceptable stand-alone policy that provides adequate coverage for the limited or excluded peril (see B7-3-05, Additional Insurance Requirements, for additional information).
elements.label: text

elements.text: The property insurance policy must provide for claims to be settled on a replacement cost basis. Property insurance policies that provide for claims to be settled on an actual cash value basis are not acceptable. Policies that limit, depreciate, reduce or otherwise settle losses at anything other than a replacement cost basis are also not acceptable.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Additional requirements apply to properties with solar panels that are leased from or owned by a third party under a power purchase agreement or other similar arrangement. See  B2-3-04, Special Property Eligibility Considerations, for additional requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: See  B7-3-08, Mortgagee Clause, Named Insured, and Notice of Cancellation Requirements for additional requirements applicable to a one- to four-unit property.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Determining the Required Coverage Amount
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender or servicer must verify that the property insurance coverage amount for a first mortgage secured by a one- to four-unit property is at least equal to the lesser of:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: 100% of the replacement cost value of the improvements as of the current property insurance policy effective date, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the unpaid principal balance of the loan, provided it equals no less than 80% of the replacement cost value of the improvements as of the current property insurance policy effective date.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The source that the lender or servicer uses to verify the coverage amount may be the property insurer, an independent insurance risk specialist, or other professional with appropriate resources to make such a determination. This may include, but is not limited to, a statement from the insurer or other applicable professional, a replacement cost estimator, or an insurance risk appraisal.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the coverage amount does not meet the minimum required, coverage that does provide the minimum required amount must be obtained.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The following table provides the steps to calculate the amount of required property insurance coverage.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Step
elements.label: text

elements.text: Description
elements.label: text

elements.text: Compare the replacement cost value of the improvements to the unpaid principal balance (UPB)
elements.label: text

elements.text: of the loan.
elements.label: text

elements.text: 1A
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the replacement cost value of the improvements is less than the UPB, the replacement cost
elements.label: text

elements.text: value is the amount of coverage required.
elements.label: text

elements.text: 1B
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the UPB of the loan is less than the replacement cost value of the improvements, go to Step 2.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Calculate 80% of the replacement cost value of the improvements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: 2A
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the result of this calculation is equal to or less than the UPB of the loan, the UPB is the amount
elements.label: text

elements.text: of coverage required.
elements.label: text

elements.text: 2B
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the result of this calculation is greater than the UPB of the loan, this calculated figure is the
elements.label: text

elements.text: amount of coverage required.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : When calculating the required amount of property insurance coverage, the loan amount must be used at origination and the UPB must be used during servicing of the loan.
elements.label: text

elements.label: text

elements.text: Deductible Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The maximum allowable deductible for all required property insurance perils for one-to four-unit properties is 5% of the property insurance coverage amount.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When a property insurance policy includes multiple deductibles, such as a separate deductible that applies to windstorms, or a separate deductible that applies to a specific property element such as the roof, the total amount for such deductibles applicable to a single occurrence must be no greater than 5% of the property insurance coverage amount.

elements.text: B7-3-03, Master Property Insurance Requirements for Project Developments (02/07/2024)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on master property insurance requirements for project developments, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Overview
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Determining if a Master Property Insurance Policy is Required
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Coverage Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Determining the Required Coverage Amount
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Deductible Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Special Coverage Requirements for Project Developments
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Special Coverage Requirements for Condo Projects
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Builder/Developer Property Insurance Policies
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Policies Covering Multiple Projects
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Overview
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic covers requirements for master property insurance policies covering the common elements and residential structures of project developments.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The requirements applicable to condo projects in this topic also apply to two- to four-unit condos and detached condos, unless stated otherwise.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For the purposes of this topic and  B7-3-04, Individual Property Insurance Requirements for a Unit in a Project Development, the following definitions apply:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Common elements refers to insurable, non-residential elements of a project development. Examples include, but are not limited to, clubhouses, parking areas or structures, and recreational facilities.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Residential structures refers to the insurable elements of a building that contains one or more residences.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Determining if a Master Property Insurance Policy is Required
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The requirements for individual property insurance policies will vary based on the homeowners' association (HOA) or co-op corporation's legal documents and the master property insurance policy. The following table provides the requirements based on the project type.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Project Type
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Requirement
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: PUD
elements.label: text

elements.text: Individual property insurance policies as described in  B7-3-02, Property Insurance Requirements
elements.label: text

elements.text: for One-to Four-Unit Properties are required for each property securing a loan that Fannie Mae
elements.label: text

elements.text: purchases in a PUD project unless the project's legal documents provide for a master property
elements.label: text

elements.text: insurance policy that covers both the common elements and residential structures. In that case,
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae will accept the master property insurance policy in satisfaction of its insurance
elements.label: text

elements.text: requirements for the subject property. The borrower may still have to maintain an individual unit
elements.label: text

elements.text: owner policy as described in
elements.label: text

elements.text: Coverage Requirements
elements.label: text

elements.text: in this topic.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When units located within a PUD are covered by a master property insurance policy maintained by
elements.label: text

elements.text: the HOA, the lender or servicer must verify that the insurance provides coverage for both the
elements.label: text

elements.text: common elements and residential structures.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When units located within a PUD are covered by individual property insurance policies maintained
elements.label: text

elements.text: by their respective owner(s), the lender or servicer is not required to verify master property
elements.label: text

elements.text: insurance coverage on PUD common elements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Condo
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Master property insurance policies are required for the common elements and residential structures
elements.label: text

elements.text: for each loan that Fannie Mae purchases in a condo project unless the condo project's legal
elements.label: text

elements.text: documents require individual property insurance policies for each unit. In that case, the individual
elements.label: text

elements.text: property insurance policy must meet the requirements in B7-3-02, Property Insurance
elements.label: text

elements.text: Requirements for One-to Four-Unit Properties.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When a master property insurance policy is required, the lender or servicer must verify that the
elements.label: text

elements.text: master property insurance provides coverage for both the common elements and residential
elements.label: text

elements.text: structures. The borrower may still have to maintain an individual unit owner policy as described in
elements.label: text

elements.text: Coverage Requirements
elements.label: text

elements.text: in this topic.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Co-op
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Master property insurance policies are required for the common elements and residential structures
elements.label: text

elements.text: for each loan that Fannie Mae purchases in a co-op project unless the co-op project's legal
elements.label: text

elements.text: documents require individual property insurance policies for each unit. In that case, the individual
elements.label: text

elements.text: property insurance policy must meet the requirements in B7-3-02, Property Insurance
elements.label: text

elements.text: Requirements for One-to Four-Unit Properties.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When a master property insurance policy is required, the lender or servicer must verify that the
elements.label: text

elements.text: master property insurance provides coverage for both the common elements and residential
elements.label: text

elements.text: structures. The borrower may still have to maintain an individual unit owner policy as described in
elements.label: text

elements.text: Coverage Requirements
elements.label: text

elements.text: in this topic.
elements.label: text

elements.text: To the extent the master property insurance policy does not cover the interior of the unit or improvements to the unit, the borrower must maintain an individual unit owner property insurance policy (see B7-3-04, Individual Property Insurance Requirements for a Unit in a Project Development  for additional requirements).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Coverage Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: When required, a master property insurance policy must be maintained with premiums paid as a common expense by the HOA or co-op corporation, as applicable. The policy must cover all insurable property elements. Common personal property and supplies should be covered, if applicable.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The master property insurance policy must provide for claims to be settled on a replacement cost basis. Property insurance policies that provide for claims to be settled on an actual cash value basis are not acceptable. Policies that limit, depreciate, reduce or otherwise settle losses at anything other than a replacement cost basis are also not acceptable.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Master property insurance coverage policies covering project developments should be written on a "Special" coverage form or equivalent. At a minimum, the coverage must include the perils covered by a commercial "Broad" coverage form, as listed in the following table.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Required Perils
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Fire
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lightning
elements.label: text

elements.text: Explosion
elements.label: text

elements.text: Windstorm (including named storms designated by the U.S. National Weather Service or the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration by a name or number)
elements.label: text

elements.text: Hail
elements.label: text

elements.text: Smoke
elements.label: text

elements.text: Aircraft or Vehicles
elements.label: text

elements.text: Riot or civil commotion
elements.label: text

elements.text: Vandalism
elements.label: text

elements.text: Sprinkler leakage
elements.label: text

elements.text: Sinkhole collapse
elements.label: text

elements.text: Volcanic action
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Required Perils
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Falling objects
elements.label: text

elements.text: Weight of snow, ice or sleet
elements.label: text

elements.text: Water damage
elements.label: text

elements.text: If a master property insurance policy excludes or limits coverage of any of the required perils, the HOA or co-op corporation must obtain an acceptable stand-alone property insurance policy which provides adequate coverage for the limited or excluded peril (see  B7-3-05, Additional Insurance Requirements for additional information).
elements.label: text

elements.text: See Named Insured for Property and Flood Insurance in  B7-3-08, Mortgagee Clause, Named Insured, and Notice of Cancellation Requirements for the named insured requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Determining the Required Coverage Amount
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender or servicer must verify that the property insurance coverage amount is at least equal to 100% of the replacement cost value of the project improvements, including common elements and residential structures, as of the current property insurance policy effective date.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The source that the lender or servicer uses to verify the coverage amount may be the property insurer, an independent insurance risk specialist, or other professional with appropriate resources to make such a determination. This may include, but is not limited to, a statement from the insurer or other applicable professional, a replacement cost estimator, or an insurance risk appraisal.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Deductible Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The following table describes the maximum allowable deductible for master property insurance policies covering project developments.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Deductible
elements.label: text

elements.text: Type
elements.label: text

elements.text: Maximum Deductible
elements.label: text

elements.text: Per
elements.label: text

elements.text: occurrence
elements.label: text

elements.text: The maximum allowable deductible for all required property insurance perils is 5% of the
elements.label: text

elements.text: master property insurance coverage amount.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Deductible Type
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Maximum Deductible
elements.label: text

elements.text: Per
elements.label: text

elements.text: occurrence,
elements.label: text

elements.text: multiple
elements.label: text

elements.text: deductibles
elements.label: text

elements.text: When a master property insurance policy includes multiple deductibles, such as a
elements.label: text

elements.text: separate deductible that applies to windstorms, or a separate deductible that applies to
elements.label: text

elements.text: a specific property element such as the roof, the total amount for such deductibles
elements.label: text

elements.text: applicable to a single occurrence must be no greater than 5% of the insurance coverage
elements.label: text

elements.text: amount.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Per occurrence, per unit
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Fannie Mae will allow a per unit master property insurance policy deductible when the
elements.label: text

elements.text: sum of the applicable per unit deductibles is greater than 5% of the coverage amount
elements.label: text

elements.text: and all of the following requirements are met.
elements.label: text

elements.text: 1. The master property insurance policy has a per unit deductible for named perils
elements.label: text

elements.text: specific to a geographic area where such coverage is common and customary; and
elements.label: text

elements.text: 2. The borrower's individual property insurance policy includes:
elements.label: text

elements.text: a. coverage for the applicable peril(s);
elements.label: text

elements.text: b. coverage for master property insurance policy deductible assessments     levied on
elements.label: text

elements.text: the unit owner by the HOA or co-op corporation for the applicable peril(s); and
elements.label: text

elements.text: c. loss assessment coverage in an amount sufficient to cover assessments in excess
elements.label: text

elements.text: of 5% of the master property insurance policy coverage amount, divided by the number
elements.label: text

elements.text: of units.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note: A deductible buy-back insurance policy purchased by the HOA or co-op corporation may be used to meet Fannie Mae's master property insurance policy deductible requirements, provided the policy meets all other property insurance requirements in Chapter B7-3, Property and Flood Insurance , including insurer rating requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Special Coverage Requirements for Project Developments
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The following special coverage requirements apply to condo, co-op, and PUD master property insurance policies:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Inflation Guard Coverage - The coverage is not required if it is not obtainable in the insurance market available to the association;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Building Ordinance or Law Coverage - The coverage must include:
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Coverage A: loss to the undamaged portion of a building,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Coverage B: demolition costs, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Coverage C: increased costs of construction.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Building Ordinance or Law Coverage may be included in the property coverage form or obtained as an endorsement to the property insurance policy. The coverage is not required if it is not obtainable in the insurance market available to the association; and
elements.label: text

elements.text: Boiler and Machinery/Equipment Breakdown Coverage - This coverage is required if the project development has central heating or cooling. The coverage amount must equal the lesser of $2 million or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: the replacement cost value of the building(s) housing the boiler or machinery. This coverage may be included in the property coverage form, obtained as an endorsement to the master property insurance policy, or the HOA or co-op corporation may purchase a stand-alone boiler and machinery policy.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note: Boiler and Machinery/Equipment Breakdown Coverage may also be referred to as Steam Boiler Coverage or Mechanical Breakdown Coverage.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Special Coverage Requirements for Condo Projects
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Master property insurance policies for condo projects must be endorsed with a Condominium Association Coverage Form or its equivalent. The endorsement must include the following provisions or comparable language:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Recognition of an Insurance Trustee: If you name an insurance trustee, we will adjust losses with you, but we will pay the insurance trustee. If we pay the trustee, the payments will satisfy your claims against us.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Waiver of Rights of Recovery: We waive our rights to recover payment from any unit-owner of the condominium that is shown in the declarations.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Unit-owner's Insurance: A unit-owner may have other insurance covering the same property as this insurance. This insurance is intended to be primary, and not to contribute with such other insurance.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Builder/Developer Property Insurance Policies
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When a project is under development, it may be covered by the builder/developer's property insurance policy if the policy provides equivalent coverage to the requirements for project developments in this topic. When property coverage ceases per the terms of the builder/developer's policy, the HOA or co-op corporation must obtain a master property insurance policy in accordance with Fannie Mae's requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Separate projects under development by the same developer will be considered affiliated during the period when control of the project has not yet transferred from the builder/developer to the individual owners or related HOA or co-op corporation. The affiliated status of the subject project ends when the property coverage ceases per the terms of the builder/developer's policy.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Policies Covering Multiple Projects
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Except as described below, unaffiliated projects may not share a master property insurance policy. Each project must maintain its own policy that meets Fannie Mae requirements, as detailed throughout this topic.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If a property insurance policy that covers multiple unaffiliated projects provides a dedicated coverage amount for each individual covered project, the policy structure may provide equivalent coverage to Fannie Mae's coverage amount requirements. The coverage amount dedicated to the subject project must be sufficient to cover the full replacement cost value of the project improvements including the common elements and residential structures.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender or servicer must review the insurance policy and any other associated documents needed to adequately evaluate the insurance coverage. The HOA or co-op corporation must be protected in the same manner as if it maintained a master property insurance policy. The coverage of each insured project cannot be affected by any actions or omissions of unaffiliated projects covered by the same policy. Additionally, all other master property insurance requirements for project developments must be met.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: The lender or servicer must document how they determined the applicable policy provides acceptable coverage as detailed above. A copy of the policy, along with the lender's or servicer's documentation must be maintained in the loan file.
elements.label: text

elements.text: B7-3-04, Individual Property Insurance Requirements for a Unit in a Project Development (12/14/2022)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on property insurance requirements for units in project developments, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Overview
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Determining if an Individual Property Insurance Policy on a Unit in a Project Development is Required
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Coverage Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Determining the Required Coverage Amount
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Deductible Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Overview
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: See Overview in  B7-3-03, Master Property Insurance Requirements for Project Developments  for additional information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Determining if an Individual Property Insurance Policy on a Unit in a Project Development is Required
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: See Determining if a Master Property Insurance Policy is Required in  B7-3-03, Master Property Insurance Requirements for Project Developments for determining when a master property insurance policy and/or an individual property insurance policy is required.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Coverage Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: See Coverage Requirements in  B7-3-02, Property Insurance Requirements for One-to Four-Unit Properties, for the coverage requirements when an individual property insurance policy is required for a unit in a project development.
elements.label: text

elements.text: See  B7-3-08, Mortgagee Clause, Named Insured, and Notice of Cancellation Requirements for requirements applicable to a loan secured by a unit in a project development.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Determining the Required Coverage Amount
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: To the extent the master property insurance policy does not cover the interior or improvements of a unit in a project development, the borrower must maintain an individual property insurance policy.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender or servicer must verify the coverage amount is sufficient to restore the unit to its condition prior to a loss event.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Coverage sufficiency should be based on the best information known or available to the lender or servicer, which may include information obtained from the borrower, in collaboration with the insurer, the HOA or co-op corporation legal documents, or other professional with appropriate resources to make such a determination.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae recommends that lenders and servicers encourage borrowers to closely collaborate with an insurance professional to determine their individual insurance needs.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Deductible Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: See Deductible Requirements in  B7-3-02, Property Insurance Requirements for One-to Four-Unit Properties for deductible requirements when an individual property insurance policy is required for a unit in a project development.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on additional insurance requirements, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Additional Property Insurance Coverage
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Insurance Requirements for Renovation and Energy-Related Improvement Loans
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Optional Credit Life or Mortgage Loan Insurance
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Additional Property Insurance Coverage
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: If a lender or servicer becomes aware of a subject property's exposure to perils that are not covered as required in Coverage Requirements in  B7-3-02, Property Insurance Requirements for One-to Four-Unit Properties, or Coverage Requirements in  B7-3-03, Master Property Insurance Requirements for Project Developments, such as toxic waste; it should contact Fannie Mae to determine whether additional coverage is necessary. Servicers should contact their Fannie Mae Servicing Representative (see Servicing Guide , F-4-02, List of Contacts) and lenders should contact their Fannie Mae Account Team (see  E-1-02, List of Contacts).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Insurance Requirements for Renovation and Energy-Related Improvement Loans
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When the loan provides funds to cover the costs of repairs, remodeling, renovations, or energy-related improvements, there must not be any coverage exclusions or limitations related to the renovation work during the time that work is being performed on the property.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Once repairs, renovation, or construction is complete, the lender or servicer must review the property insurance policy (and flood insurance policy when required) to ensure the appropriate coverage is in place based on the completed replacement cost value of the subject property.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Optional Credit Life or Mortgage Loan Insurance
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Fannie Mae allows insurance policies that include optional coverage such as those outlined below. However, Fannie Mae does not pay costs arising from disputes with insurers in settling claims that relate only to this optional coverage.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Acceptable Types of Optional Credit Life or Mortgage Loan Insurance
elements.label: text

elements.text: Permissible coverage includes:
elements.label: text

elements.text: single-premium credit insurance policies and debt cancellation agreements that are free in all respects to the borrower,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: single-premium mortgage insurance policies with a credit insurance feature where such credit insurance feature is free in all respects to the borrower, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: credit insurance policies that require borrowers to pay a separately identified premium on a monthly or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: annual basis or debt cancellation agreements that require borrowers to pay a separately identified fee on a monthly basis.
elements.label: text

elements.text: These credit insurance policies or debt cancellation agreements must be disclosed to the borrower in clear and simple terms in advance of purchase of the applicable policy or agreement.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Notwithstanding the lender's or servicer's compliance with the above requirements, Fannie Mae purchases loans with debt cancellation agreements only with Fannie Mae's express written approval of the overall debt cancellation feature. This includes the debt cancellation agreement, and execution by the lender or servicer and Fannie Mae of a separately negotiated agreement.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender or servicer may act as a broker or agent in the sale of this type of credit insurance to the borrower.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender or servicer must reimburse Fannie Mae for attorney's fees or any costs that it incurs if Fannie Mae brings an action on a defaulted loan and the borrower defends against Fannie Mae's foreclosure or acts to enjoin Fannie Mae from liquidating the loan and one of the defenses or actions for injunction is based on:
elements.label: text

elements.text: an obligation of the lender or servicer (including as the broker or agent that obtained the credit insurance for the borrower and/or as a party that has agreed to collect premiums and remit them to the credit insurer on the borrower's behalf),
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: an obligation of the credit insurer, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the obligation of the mortgage insurer to maintain credit insurance and apply benefits thereof to the borrower's loan.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Unacceptable Types of Optional Credit Life or Mortgage Loan Insurance
elements.label: text

elements.text: Although certain property insurance policies that include optional coverage are allowed, Fannie Mae does not purchase loans in the following situations:
elements.label: text

elements.text: The premium/fee for single-premium credit life insurance policies or debt cancellation agreements is paid directly by the borrower or paid indirectly by financing the premium/fee into the loan amount.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The premium/fee for single-premium mortgage insurance policies with a credit insurance feature is paid directly by the borrower or paid indirectly by rolling the credit insurance single premium into the cost of the mortgage insurance (whether or not it is identified as including a credit insurance premium).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Determining if a Property Requires Flood Insurance
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender and servicer must ensure that the subject property is adequately protected by flood insurance when required. Flood insurance coverage is required when a loan is secured by a property located in
elements.label: text

elements.text: a Special Flood Hazard Area (SFHA), or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a Coastal Barrier Resources System (CBRS) or Otherwise Protected Area (OPA).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The lender and servicer must determine whether the property is located in an SFHA, a CBRS, or an OPA by using the FEMA Standard Flood Hazard Determination form. All flood zones beginning with the letter "A" or "V" are considered SFHAs.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note: If the subject property is located within a CBRS or an OPA, flood insurance is required regardless of whether the property is located in an SFHA.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Flood insurance is not required on a principal or residential detached structure securing the loan if the lender or servicer obtains a letter from FEMA stating that its maps have been amended and the structure is no longer in an SFHA.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Community Eligibility
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: If the lender or servicer determines that a principal or residential detached structure is located in an SFHA, but the community does not participate in the National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP), the loan is not eligible for purchase by Fannie Mae.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the property is in a non-participating community and a CBRS or an OPA, it is only eligible for purchase by Fannie Mae if it is not located in an SFHA.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Loans secured by properties in the Emergency Program of the NFIP are eligible for purchase by Fannie Mae with coverage equivalent to the NFIP maximum that is available.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Acceptable Flood Insurance Policies
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The flood insurance policy must be one of the following:
elements.label: text

elements.text: a standard policy issued under the NFIP; or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a policy issued by a private insurer, provided
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the terms and amount of coverage are at least equal to that provided under an NFIP policy based on a review of the full policy issued by a private insurer, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: the insurer meets Fannie Mae's rating requirements as specified in Property Insurer Rating Requirements in  B7-3-01, General Property Insurance Requirements for All Property Types.
elements.label: text

elements.text: A Policy Declaration page is acceptable evidence of flood insurance.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Requirements for One- to Four-Unit Properties
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The minimum amount of flood insurance required for first mortgages must be equal to the lesser of
elements.label: text

elements.text: 100% of the replacement cost value of the improvements,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the maximum coverage amount available from NFIP, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the unpaid principal balance (UPB) of the loan (or loan amount at the time of origination).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: See Insurance Requirements for Renovation and Energy Improvement Loans in  B7-3-05, Additional Insurance Requirements  for additional information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: See Mortgagee Clause for Property and Flood Insurance and Named Insured for Property and Flood Insurance in B7-3-08, Mortgagee Clause, Named Insured, and Notice of Cancellation Requirements for mortgagee clause and named insured requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Requirements During a National Flood Insurance Program Lapse
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The NFIP may lapse due to a shutdown of the federal government or if Congress does not renew the NFIP's authorization to issue new policies, increase coverage on existing policies, or issue renewal policies.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: For the duration of such a lapse, a lender may sell a loan to Fannie Mae where flood insurance is otherwise required without an active flood insurance policy provided the requirements below are met.
elements.label: text

elements.text: During the lapse, lenders must have a process in place to identify properties securing loans sold to us without proper evidence of active flood insurance on the conditions that the borrower must provide acceptable evidence of:
elements.label: text

elements.text: a completed application for NFIP flood insurance and proof of the premium payment or the final settlement statement reflecting payment of the initial premium, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the assignment of an existing NFIP flood insurance policy from the property seller to the purchaser.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The following table provides additional requirements that lenders and servicers must follow during the lapse.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lenders and servicers must...
elements.label: text

elements.text: Continue to make flood determinations, provide timely, complete and accurate notices to
elements.label: text

elements.text: borrowers in accordance with applicable laws, and comply with all other flood insurance
elements.label: text

elements.text: requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Take all steps (as permitted by applicable law) necessary to facilitate the issuance of coverage
elements.label: text

elements.text: once the lapse has ended.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Retain documentation to support acceptable evidence of flood insurance.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Refinance loans secured by properties where flood insurance is required typically already have acceptable flood insurance coverage in place at the time of closing. Specific requirements for refinance loans are listed below.

elements.label: text

elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: When delivering loans affected by a lapse of the NFIP, lenders must follow the Delivery Requirements described above and provide all applicable loan delivery data elements as if a required flood insurance policy was in place.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender and servicer remain obligated for all selling representations and warranties and servicing obligations concerning the existence of a standard policy issued under the NFIP or an equivalent private flood insurance policy.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : Fannie Mae accepts flood insurance policies from private insurers as listed in Acceptable Flood Insurance Policies , but such private flood insurance policy coverage is not a required alternative in the event of a lapse of NFIP coverage.

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on evidence of property insurance, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Overview
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Certificates of Property Insurance
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Master Property Insurance Policies for Project Developments
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Electronic Data Files
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Overview
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The following requirements apply when documenting and retaining evidence of property insurance policies:
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender or servicer must verify the existence of a valid insurance policy that meets Fannie Mae's requirements. Information related to the policy should be passed on to the servicer, when applicable.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The lender or servicer must retain acceptable evidence of insurance for each required policy, which includes sufficient information about the insurance policy, the subject property, and the borrower to ensure compliance with Fannie Mae's requirements for a first mortgage, unless the lender or servicer is covered by a mortgage impairment or mortgagee interest insurance policy or uses other evidence of insurance that Fannie Mae considers acceptable.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The servicer of a second mortgage must retain a copy of the insurance policy, any endorsements to it, and evidence of premium payments.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The servicer must verify at least annually that any property insurance policy for a one- to four-unit property or a unit in a project development meets Fannie Mae's requirements.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Note: If a lender is unable to determine whether the applicable insurance policy meets Fannie Mae's requirements, the associated loan is not eligible for purchase by Fannie Mae.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Certificates of Property Insurance
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A lender or servicer may accept a certificate of property insurance in lieu of a complete property insurance policy if the certificate includes all of the information needed for the lender or servicer to determine whether the insurance meets Fannie Mae's requirements. The certificate of property insurance must be signed by the insurer.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Master Property Insurance Policies for Project Developments
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When required, the lender or servicer must obtain a copy of the current master property insurance policy or a certificate of property insurance showing that the subject unit is covered under the master property insurance policy. See Servicing Guide , B-2-03, Master Property Insurance Requirements for Project Developments, for additional servicer responsibilities related to evidence of master property insurance policies for project developments.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Electronic Data Files
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Electronic data files are acceptable in lieu of hard copy insurance policies if all the requirements in the following table are met.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Requirements for Electronic Data Files for Insurance Policies
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The data file must include sufficient information about the insurance policy, the subject property, and the borrower to ensure that the servicer is able to comply with Fannie Mae's requirements for maintaining and monitoring property insurance, such as
elements.label: text

elements.text: reviewing the insurance policy terms, amount of coverage, and deductibles;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: confirming that the premiums have been paid; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: processing loss drafts.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The lender's or servicer's errors and omissions insurance policy must acknowledge electronic data transfers and fully protect the lender or servicer and Fannie Mae against losses incurred as the result of erroneous data files or transfers.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The insurer must provide the lender or servicer with written assurance that the data file is equivalent to a printed policy, typically through a detailed agreement between the two parties.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender or servicer must have appropriate procedures in place to mitigate risks associated with not possessing an original hardcopy policy, which may include obtaining certifications from the insurer as to the accuracy of certain information that the servicer is required to verify.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender or servicer must provide legible hard copies of the complete insurance policies and proof of premium payments if Fannie Mae requests them.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Mortgagee Clause for Property and Flood Insurance
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The following requirements apply to individual property and flood insurance policies maintained by the borrower and do not apply to master property and flood insurance policies for project developments.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae must not be named in the mortgagee clause unless the coverage or its interest would be impaired by Fannie Mae not being named. If Fannie Mae is named, the clause must read: "Fannie Mae, in care of (insert servicer's name and address here)." This ensures that all matters related to the applicable insurance policy are referred directly to the servicer and not to Fannie Mae.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When Fannie Mae is not named in the mortgagee clause, the lender's name, followed by the phrase "its successors and/or assigns," and mailing address must be shown as the mortgagee. If the lender is not the servicer, the servicer's name, followed by "its successors and/or assigns," and mailing address must be specified. In all cases, the insurer must be instructed to send all correspondence, policies, and bills to the servicer (or to both the first and second mortgage servicers).
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the mortgage is registered with MERS and is originated naming MERS as the original mortgagee of record, MERS must not be named as mortgagee or loss payee on any property insurance policy. The mortgagee clause must name the servicer (unless Fannie Mae must be named because the coverage or its interest would be impaired as mentioned above).

elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This chapter describes the requirements for liability and fidelity/crime insurance for project developments.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: B7-4-01, General Liability Insurance Requirements for Project Developments (12/14/2022)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on general liability insurance requirements, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Determining if General Liability Insurance is Required
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Coverage Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Determining the Required Coverage Amount
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Named Insured
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Determining if General Liability Insurance is Required
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: General liability insurance is required for all condo and co-op projects, with the following exceptions:
elements.label: text

elements.text: projects that qualify for a waiver of project review as described in  B4-2.1-02, Waiver of Project Review, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: condo projects reviewed under the Limited Review method as described in  B4-2.2-01, Limited Review Process.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Coverage Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When required, the HOA or co-op corporation must maintain a general liability insurance policy for the entire project. The general liability insurance policy must include coverage for:
elements.label: text

elements.text: commercial spaces that are owned by the HOA or co-op corporation, even if they are leased to others, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: bodily injury and property damage that results from the operation, maintenance, or the use of the project's common elements, and any other areas that are under its supervision.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The general liability insurance policy must include a separation of insureds or severability of interests provision in its terms. If the policy does not include separation of insureds or severability of interests in its terms, Fannie Mae requires a specific endorsement to preclude the insurer's denial of a unit owner's claim because of negligent acts of the HOA or co-op corporation or of other unit owners.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Determining the Required Coverage Amount
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The amount of coverage must be at least $1 million for bodily injury and property damage for any single occurrence.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Named Insured
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The general liability insurance policy must designate the HOA or co-op corporation as the named insured with the premiums paid as a common expense by the HOA or co-op corporation.
elements.label: text

elements.label: text

elements.text: Determining if Fidelity/Crime Insurance is Required
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Coverage Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Determining the Required Coverage Amount
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Named Insured
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Determining if Fidelity/Crime Insurance is Required
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Fidelity/crime insurance is required for all condo and co-op projects, with the following exceptions:
elements.label: text

elements.text: projects that qualify for a waiver of project review as described in  B4-2.1-02, Waiver of Project Review,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: condo projects reviewed under the Limited Review method as described in  B4-2.2-01, Limited Review Process,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: condo or co-op projects consisting of 20 units or less, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: condo or co-op projects that would need fidelity/crime insurance coverage of $5,000 or less (based on the calculations described in Determining the Required Coverage Amount ).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Coverage Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When required, the HOA or co-op corporation must have fidelity/crime insurance coverage for the dishonest or fraudulent acts of anyone who either handles or is responsible for funds held or administered for the HOA or coop corporation. Fidelity/crime insurance is required whether or not that individual receives compensation for services rendered. Accordingly, the HOA or co-op corporation fidelity/crime insurance policy must include coverage for the acts of any management agent.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: A management agent that handles funds for the HOA or co-op corporation should additionally be covered by its own fidelity/crime insurance policy, however, a fidelity/crime insurance policy maintained by the management agent (with the management agent as the named insured) is not an acceptable alternative for a fidelity/crime insurance policy in the HOA or co-op corporation's name that provides coverage for the acts of all parties with access to their funds, including the management agent.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Determining the Required Coverage Amount
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When fidelity/crime insurance is required, the lender or servicer must review the project legal documents or other source acceptable to the lender or servicer to verify whether the HOA or co-op corporation and any associated management company adhere to one or more of the financial controls listed below:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Separate bank accounts are maintained for the working account and the reserve account, each with appropriate access controls, and the bank in which funds are deposited sends copies of the monthly bank statements directly to the HOA or co-op corporation.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The management company maintains separate records and bank accounts for each HOA or co-op corporation that uses its services, and the management company does not have the authority to draw checks on, or transfer funds from, the reserve account of the HOA or co-op corporation.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Two members of the Board of Directors must sign any checks written on the reserve account.

elements.text: Subpart B8, Closing: Legal Documents
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: text

elements.text: This subpart describes legal document requirements in connection with mortgage loans sold to Fannie Mae.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Chapter B8-1, General Information on Legal Documents
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: text

elements.text: This chapter provides general information on Fannie Mae's requirements for legal documents.
elements.label: text

elements.text: B8-1-01, Publication of Legal Documents (06/28/2011)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on settlement evidence, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Publication of Legal Documents
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Legal Documents for Government Mortgages
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Publication of Legal Documents
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Fannie Mae publishes Legal Documents  on its website. These legal documents include security instruments, notes, riders and addenda, and special-purpose documents that should be used in connection with regularly amortizing, conventional, residential mortgage loans sold to Fannie Mae. Many of these forms are published jointly by Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac and are referred to as Uniform Instruments.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Each legal document published on Fannie Mae's website is accompanied by a Summary or Instructions
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: document, which provides:
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: the latest revision date for the document,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the document's purpose and type of mortgage transactions for which it is used,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: instructions on how to print the electronic version of the document,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: specific changes that must be made to the document,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: additional changes that may be made to the document, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: other pertinent information about special circumstances that may affect the user or completion of the document.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Legal Documents for Government Mortgages
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A lender should use legal documents for regularly amortizing FHA-insured mortgages, VA-guaranteed mortgages, RD-guaranteed mortgages, and HUD-guaranteed mortgages that are acceptable to the government agency and are appropriate for the state in which the security property is located.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Although Fannie Mae does not publish documents for government mortgages, in some cases Fannie Mae allows (or requires) its legal documents to be used. If a lender chooses to use Fannie Mae's legal documents for conventional mortgages, and if those documents must be modified or amended to comply with applicable government agency requirements, the following requirements must be met:
elements.label: text

elements.text: The documents must be enforceable under their terms.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The documents must comply with all applicable state and local requirements for a recordable and enforceable document.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The lender must make nonstandard document warranties that are similar to those Fannie Mae requires for other mortgages closed on documents other than Fannie Mae's standard documents. See A2-2-03, Document Warranties.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Recent Related Announcements
elements.label: text

elements.text: There are no recently issued Announcements related to this topic.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Chapter B8-2, Security Instruments
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: text

elements.text: This chapter provides information on security instruments.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: B8-2-01, Security Instruments for Conventional Mortgages (02/06/2019)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains general information on conventional first mortgage security instruments, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Use of Security Instruments
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Standard First-Lien Mortgage Security Instruments
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Master Form and Short Form Documents
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Use of Security Instruments
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders must use security instruments for conventional mortgages that are correct for the applicable jurisdiction, mortgage type, lien type, property type, and transaction type. Security instruments for regularly amortizing mortgages include the Fannie Mae/Freddie Mac Uniform Mortgages, Deeds of Trust, and Security Deeds.
elements.label: text

elements.text: In some cases, the uniform security instruments may have to be adapted to meet the lender's needs or local jurisdictional requirements. If, however, a security instrument is modified in any way, Fannie Mae will consider it to be a nonstandard document, which means the lender's delivery of the loan is subject to Fannie Mae's requirements for nonstandard documents. For more information on the warranties a lender makes when it sells Fannie Mae mortgages closed on nonstandard documents, see A2-2-03, Document Warranties.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Standard First-Lien Mortgage Security Instruments
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The current versions of the Fannie Mae standard first mortgage security instruments are posted on Fannie Maes's Legal Documents  website. The website also contains instructions for use of these documents, including any required changes and some changes that may be made at lender's option, called 'authorized changes.' Lenders must make sure that any changes they make, including changes authorized by Fannie Mae, comply with all applicable laws.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The standard uniform security instruments are used for almost all types of regularly amortizing mortgages. In some instances, the standard security instruments must be modified by a rider. For example, a rider is needed for an adjustable-rate mortgage as well as for a security property that is a one-unit dwelling that is used as a second home or an investment property, or that is a two- to four-unit property or a unit in a PUD or condo project. For more information about these riders, see B8-4-01, Riders and Addenda.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Master Form and Short Form Documents
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders may elect to deliver first-lien mortgage loans to Fannie Mae using a master form mortgage or deed of trust ('Master Form') and short form mortgage or deed of trust ('Short Form') in states with statutes that allow for the use of these forms. The uniform Master Form and Short Form documents may be used in lieu of the current version of the Fannie Mae/Freddie Mac uniform first mortgage security instruments.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Under applicable state law, lenders may record a Master Form in a given recording jurisdiction, and then may subsequently record a Short Form for any mortgage loan originated in that jurisdiction.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The Master Form consists of a title page, which contains the state-specific requirements for a master security instrument, and the current long form uniform security instrument for that state. The Short Form contains the loan-specific information (for example, borrower name, lender name, loan amount, description of property, etc.) and identifies the provisions of the Master Form that are being incorporated into the Short Form. Any applicable riders to the security instrument must be attached to the Short Form. The borrower must execute the Short Form document and any applicable riders. The lender must provide the borrower with a copy of the recorded Master Form as well as the signed Short Form and any applicable riders.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The documents are available for 27 states and are posted on Fannie Mae's  Legal Documents website.
elements.label: text

elements.text: This topic contains general information on special-purpose security instruments, including:

elements.text: General Information
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Consolidated New York Mortgages

elements.text: General Information
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Fannie Mae sometimes allows special-purpose alternative documents to be used in lieu of (or in addition to) the typical security instruments. These documents can be found on Fannie Mae's  Legal Documents website. Authorized changes that must or may be made to those documents are set out in the instructions that accompany each document. These instruments must be supported by the appropriate mortgage riders, rider addenda, mortgage assignments, and, if applicable, other product-specific documentation (see B8-4, Riders and Addenda, and B8-6, Mortgage Assignments).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Consolidated New York Mortgages
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The statutory provisions of New York permit refinance mortgages (and sometimes purchase money mortgages) to be documented by a consolidation, extension, and modification agreement (CEMA) that consolidates into one document the terms of prior notes and mortgages related to the security property and, if new funds are advanced, the terms of a new note and mortgage. In such instances, the consolidation must be documented on Fannie Mae's standard Consolidation, Extension and Modification Agreement (Form 3172), along with any accompanying exhibits Fannie Mae may specify. If new funds are advanced, Fannie Mae's standard security instrument must be used to document the new mortgage that is being consolidated with the prior mortgages.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Puerto Rico Direct Mortgage Instruments
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The statutory provisions of Puerto Rico permit a mortgage transaction to be documented by a single instrument that combines the terms of a note and mortgage. This is referred to as a 'direct' mortgage. Fannie Mae does not publish standard legal documents for direct mortgages; therefore, lenders must develop (or acquire) appropriate documentation for these mortgages consistent with the applicable Puerto Rico statutes. By delivering a direct mortgage to Fannie Mae, the lender must make the nonstandard document warranties. (See A2-2-02, Delivery Information and Delivery-Option Specific Representations and Warranties.)
elements.label: text

elements.text: Security Instruments for Manufactured Home Mortgages
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Fannie Mae prefers lenders to use the standard Fannie Mae uniform instruments for manufactured home loans sold to it.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Loan documents are not acceptable if they:
elements.label: text

elements.text: state that the home is personal property or contain other words to that effect;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: state that the parties do not intend to attach the home to a permanent foundation system on the land, or contain statements inconsistent with that intention;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: unless required by law, provide that rights of holders in due course are waived, or with other words provide that an assignee note holder may be held liable for claims the borrower may have against other parties; or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: include consumer finance paper (which combines the note and security instrument in a single document) or a retail installment sales contract.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The following list provides the requirements for the security instrument used for a manufactured home loan.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The property description section of the security instrument must include a comprehensive description of the manufactured home and the land. The description must include the serial or VIN number (or the serial number or VIN for each unit if the home is multi-width), make, model, size, and any other information that may be required by applicable law to definitively identify the home. The serial number is located on the HUD Data Plate located on the interior of the home, usually near the electrical box. In addition, the serial number is generally cold stamped on the frame front cross member of each transportable section.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Some jurisdictions may not allow any information in the property description section of the security instrument other than what is customary for other real property transactions. If this is the case, then an addendum may be used, which must be attached to the security instrument and included in the loan file.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: The security instrument must state that the manufactured home is an improvement to the land and an immovable fixture, or must include similar language as may be required by applicable law to assure, to the greatest extent possible, that the manufactured home is treated as real property under applicable state law. If applicable law provides specific obligatory wording, such wording must be used.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The borrower(s) and any lender with a personal property security interest in the manufactured home must sign an Affidavit of Affixture that acknowledges their intent for the manufactured home to be permanently part of the real property that secures the mortgage free of any personal property security interest. It must also contain any specific language that may be required by applicable law.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The Affidavit must be signed by both the lender and the borrower(s), preferably recorded, and must be retained in the loan file.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Failure to include the Affidavit of Affixture in the loan file may result in the loan being ineligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If state law requires a Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) filing in order to perfect a security interest in a manufactured home, the lender must make such filing in any and all appropriate locations.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Uniform Appraisal Dataset (UAD) 3.6 Policy
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders using UAD 3.6 must follow the requirements in the UAD 3.6 Policy Supplement .
elements.label: text

elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Borrowers' Signatures on Security Instruments
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Signature Requirements: Powers of Attorney and Guardianship
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Borrowers' Signatures on Security Instruments
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The following person(s) must sign the security instrument:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Each person who has an ownership interest in the security property, even if the person's income is not used in qualifying for the loan.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The spouse or domestic partner of any person who has an interest in the property, if their signature is necessary under applicable state law to waive any property right they have by virtue of being the owner's spouse or domestic partner.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Signature Requirements: Powers of Attorney and Guardianship
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The following persons may sign security instruments on a borrower's behalf:
elements.label: text

elements.text: An attorney-in-fact may sign the security instrument, as long as the lender obtains a copy of the applicable power of attorney. In jurisdictions where a power of attorney used for a signature on a security instrument must be recorded with the security instrument, the lender must ensure that recordation has been effected. See B8-5-05, Requirements for Use of a Power of Attorney, for further requirements governing the use of a power of attorney.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: A court-appointed guardian may sign the security instrument if the borrower is not legally competent, provided that they have unlimited power over the ward's affairs, including the power to hold, convey, and give a lien against real property owned by the ward, to make payments from the ward's assets, and to permit inquiries concerning the ward's credit. The lender should obtain a copy of the documents making the appointment. If the guardian in some other capacity is a party to the loan or sale transaction-for example, the seller of the property-the lender should ascertain that there are no material conflicts of interest.
elements.label: list_item

elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: text

elements.text: This chapter describes requirements for conventional first and second mortgages notes, special note provisions, and signature and endorsement requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: B8-3-01, Notes for Conventional Mortgages (09/02/2020)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on notes for conventional loans, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Use of Conventional First Mortgage Notes
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Adjustable-Rate Mortgage Notes
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Use of Conventional First Mortgage Notes
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders should use the note that is correct for the applicable mortgage type, lien type, property type, and product type for regularly amortizing conventional mortgages that are closed on the Fannie Mae/Freddie Mac uniform security instruments.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae publishes state-specific fixed-rate notes for ten jurisdictions (although they are not available for all products). The multistate note can be used in most jurisdictions unless the security property is located in a jurisdiction for which Fannie Mae publishes a state-specific mortgage note.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The various notes that are used for fixed-rate mortgages may be found on Fannie Mae's  Legal Documents website. Authorized changes that must or may be made to these documents are set out in the instructions that accompany each document.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Adjustable-Rate Mortgage Notes
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Generally, notes for adjustable-rate mortgages apply to specific ARM plans. Fannie Mae does not publish statespecific notes for all ARM plans. If Fannie Mae does not publish state-specific documents for a particular ARM plan, the lender must use the multistate note for that particular ARM plan and amend it as necessary to satisfy the requirements of the jurisdiction in which the security property is located. Required amendments may be incorporated by reprinting the amended documents, adding addenda to the documents, or making the changes on the documents themselves. The various notes that are used for ARMs may be found on Fannie Mae's Legal Documents website. Authorized changes that must or may be made to these documents are set out in the instructions that accompany each document.

elements.text: B8-3-02, Special Note Provisions and Language Requirements (06/03/2020)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on special note provisions and language requirements, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Late Charges for Conventional Mortgages
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Requirement for Unit Number
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Mandatory Arbitration
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Late Charges for Conventional Mortgages
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The note for a conventional first mortgage must include information about the late charge to be assessed for any payment that is not received by the 15th day after it becomes due. The amount of the late charge must meet the following requirements:
elements.label: text

elements.text: The amount must be permissible under applicable law.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The late charge must be a minimum of 0% and up to 5% of the principal and interest (P&I) payment.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Requirement for Unit Number
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: If the subject property is a condo or other property type that is identified by a unit number, the unit number must be included in the property address on the note.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Mandatory Arbitration
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Mandatory arbitration is a loan provision or an agreement accompanying the loan that requires the borrower to submit to arbitration any disputes arising out of or relating in any way to the mortgage transaction. Mortgages that are subject to mandatory arbitration are ineligible for sale to, or securitization by, Fannie Mae unless the mandatory arbitration provision provides that, in the event of a transfer or sale of the mortgage or an interest in the mortgage to Fannie Mae, the mandatory arbitration clause immediately and automatically becomes null and void and cannot be reinstated.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender must provide the borrower with written notice of the waiver provision within 60 days of the transfer or sale of the mortgage to Fannie Mae. That notice must contain substantially the following language:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Pursuant to your mortgage documents, Fannie Mae is hereby notifying you that an interest in your loan has been transferred or sold to Fannie Mae and therefore the mandatory arbitration clause of your loan, requiring that you submit to arbitration to resolve any dispute arising out of or relating in any way to your mortgage, is immediately null and void.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: The lender and servicer shall maintain a copy of such notice in the mortgage file.
elements.label: text
